text: When asked how she overcame setbacks early in her career, Chef Amara Osei pauses before answering.
Growing up in Accra, she had always felt a keen appetite for cooking, but the culinary industry was dominated by men who dismissed her ambitions.
Rather than conform to expectations, she chose to pursue her goal with fierce determination.
She trained in Paris, where she endured harsh criticism from instructors who doubted her potential.
Looking back, she says those experiences were invaluable.
They taught her to distinguish between constructive feedback and mere prejudice.
Today, Osei runs three restaurants across West Africa, each one a tribute to indigenous ingredients and traditional techniques.
Her philosophy is straightforward: use what the land provides, and never substitute quality for convenience.
When asked about the widespread belief that African cuisine lacks sophistication, she responds sharply.
The world is only just beginning to grasp the depth and diversity of what this continent offers, she says.
And I intend to make sure they pay attention.
